symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden unilateral hearing loss immediately after water exposure

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sudden complete blockage after water exposure fits hygroscopic swelling of pre-existing wax — but a conductive pattern can only be confirmed on examination.

Sudden SNHL presents identically at the level of symptom report; it cannot be distinguished from conductive loss without tuning-fork testing/audiometry, so its presence in the differential mandates examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: Complete blocked/plugged sensation in the ear

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A sensation of total occlusion is the cardinal symptom of a canal fully obstructed by an expanded wax plug.

symptoms.symptom_name: High-pitched tinnitus in the affected ear

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Tinnitus commonly accompanies a sudden conductive block as the occluded ear amplifies internal/somatic sounds.
Tinnitus also accompanies SSNHL, so on its own it does not discriminate conductive from sensorineural loss.
A hard plug pressing on the canal walls produces pressure-type pain; severe or worsening pain with fever would instead point to infection.
Mild imbalance can accompany a blocked ear; the key discrimination is that it is NOT true room-spinning vertigo, which would argue for inner-ear or central pathology and raise the escalation tier.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dry reflex cough on ear symptoms (Arnold's ear-cough reflex)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A dry cough triggered by canal irritation reflects Arnold's reflex (auricular branch of the vagus), indicating mechanical stimulation of the ear canal by an impacted plug or foreign material.

symptoms.symptom_name: Recent cotton-bud use in the affected ear

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Cotton-bud use is a well-recognised cause of deeper, harder impaction by pushing wax against the eardrum, and abrades the canal.

symptoms.symptom_name: Preceding weeks of ear fullness and pruritus

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A background of chronic fullness and itch indicates pre-existing cerumen accumulation that then acutely expanded with water.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever, no purulent/foul discharge, no preceding URTI, no trauma

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever, discharge and a viral prodrome makes acute suppurative middle-ear infection less likely.
No fever or post-auricular pain/swelling argues against mastoiditis.

symptoms.symptom_name: No true vertigo, no facial weakness, no bleeding/discharge from the ear

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: These negatives lower the probability of complicated inner-ear/central or destructive disease but do NOT exclude SSNHL, which is excluded only by tuning-fork testing/audiometry.

symptoms.symptom_name: Weber tuning-fork test lateralises to the affected (right) ear

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Weber lateralising TO the affected ear indicates a conductive hearing loss, consistent with canal obstruction.

In SSNHL the Weber lateralises AWAY from the affected ear; lateralisation to the affected ear effectively excludes a sensorineural cause. This is the single bedside test that resolves the differential — and it cannot be done over chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Otoscopy: right canal completely occluded by a large, hard, dark-brown cerumen plug; eardrum not visualised; mild reactive canal erythema; left eardrum pearly/intact

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Direct visualisation of a complete, hard cerumen plug obstructing the canal confirms the diagnosis; inability to see the eardrum means a perforation cannot be excluded until the wax is cleared.

symptoms.symptom_name: Afebrile with otherwise unremarkable vital signs; cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact, no facial droop

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Being afebrile with intact cranial nerves and no facial weakness argues against a complicated infective or neurological process.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: A complete blocked sensation, an Arnold's reflex dry cough, recent cotton-bud use and weeks of preceding fullness together point strongly to a mechanical cerumen obstruction — but the cluster raises probability, it does not substitute for the otoscopy/Weber examination that excludes SSNHL.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Otoscopic examination of the ear canal and tympanic membrane
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The single most important step — visualises the obstruction and the eardrum, confirms cerumen and assesses for perforation. Cannot be performed over chat; requires prompt in-person examination.
Bedside discrimination of conductive from sensorineural loss; lateralisation to the affected ear confirms a conductive cause and excludes SSNHL. Cannot be done via telehealth.
Quantifies and characterises the hearing loss; becomes urgent if hearing does not recover after wax removal, to exclude an underlying sensorineural component.

Contraindicated on a hard, completely obstructing plug and while tympanic-membrane integrity is unknown — risks pain, canal trauma and middle-ear injury if the drum is perforated. Soften with cerumenolytic drops first, then remove by micro-suction or syringing once appropriate.
